key_risk_driver: Swiss Supply Chain Due Diligence and Transparency Act (Sorgfaltspflicht und Transparenzgesetz, effective Jan 2022) improving government response sub-score; domestic trafficking enforcement improving
context: Switzerland's Tier 2 classification is driven primarily by limitations in domestic trafficking prosecution capacity and domestic worker/sex work trafficking concerns, not by high structural vulnerability (score 14). overall_risk_trajectory: Tier 2 – Stable; EU membership floor prevents deterioration

overall_risk_trajectory: Tier 2 WL – Automatic Tier 3 downgrade risk under TVPA consecutive-year rules; Secretary of State waiver required to prevent
key_risk_driver: Third consecutive Tier 2 Watchlist year triggers automatic Tier 3 downgrade under TVPA rules unless Secretary of State issues one-time waiver; government capacity deteriorating structurally
context: South Africa has been on the Tier 2 Watchlist since at least 2023. Under TVPA provisions, a country that remains on the Watchlist for 3 consecutive years is automatically downgraded to Tier 3 unless the Secretary of State certifies a credible written plan. South Africa's government response capacity is under severe structural pressure: 33%+ unemployment, ANC governance deterioration, corruption undermining criminal justice mechanisms (Walk Free criminal justice sub-score), and energy crisis reducing monitoring infrastructure. If downgraded to Tier 3 within 12 months, cluster migration from 2 (High) toward 0 (Very High) becomes a realistic medium-term scenario.
monitoring_signals: 2025 TIP Report tier decision for South Africa; Secretary of State TVPA waiver status; SAPS (South African Police Service) anti-trafficking budget and prosecution statistics; Walk Free vulnerability sub-scores for inequality and governance

overall_risk_trajectory: Tier 1 – Maintained for now; vulnerability score upward pressure
key_risk_driver: Milei government's radical economic restructuring (peso devaluation, austerity, removal of social protections) creating acute short-term vulnerability pressure on Walk Free sub-scores
context: Argentina's Tier 1 status and cluster 1 (Low) classification are currently stable. However, the Milei administration's shock therapy economic programme is creating conditions that Walk Free's vulnerability framework captures: 'lack of basic needs' (poverty rate rose from ~40% to ~55% by mid-2024), 'inequality' (social protection dismantlement), and 'disenfranchised groups' (informal economy expansion). The current TIP data predates the full Milei impact; the next Walk Free assessment cycle (data captured 2025-26) may reflect material vulnerability score deterioration. Government response score (58) and Tier 1 status are unlikely to change within 12 months absent a specific trafficking enforcement failure.
monitoring_signals: Argentine poverty and inequality statistics (INDEC); ILO Argentina country assessments; Walk Free 2027 Argentina vulnerability sub-scores; UNICEF Argentina social protection coverage data

Vulnerability score of 7 is among the lowest in the dataset. Home Affairs anti-trafficking enforcement maintaining Tier 1 classification. Independent review of MSA (2023) recommendations being considered for implementation.
monitoring_signals: Australian Government response to 2023 MSA Review recommendations; Home Affairs anti-trafficking enforcement statistics; Attorney-General modern slavery inquiry progress

overall_risk_trajectory: Tier 2 – Improving; TITP replacement directly addresses primary Tier 2 driver
key_risk_driver: Technical Intern Training Program (TITP) replacement with new Specified Skilled Worker system from 2024 directly addresses the primary driver of Japan's Tier 2 classification
context: Japan's Tier 2 status has been almost entirely driven by the TITP, which created structural conditions for debt bondage and exploitation among the ~300,000 annual technical intern trainees. The Diet approved the replacement 'Ikusei Shuuro' (育成就労) system in June 2024, abolishing TITP by 2027 and establishing genuine worker mobility rights. This is a material structural change in government response. Walk Free government response sub-scores are expected to improve in the 2027 cycle. Low structural vulnerability (11).
monitoring_signals: Implementation progress of Ikusei Shuuro system; TITP exploitation complaint data under the old system; 2025 TIP Report Japan tier assessment; Ministry of Justice enforcement statistics

overall_risk_trajectory: Tier 2 – Stable; Lista Suja enforcement showing improvement
key_risk_driver: Lula government reinvigorating Lista Suja (Slave Labour Dirty List) enforcement; MTE inspection activity increased from Bolsonaro-era lows; ILO Brazil engagement strengthened
context: Brazil has one of the most sophisticated domestic forced labour enforcement mechanisms globally (Lista Suja, Special Mobile Inspection Group). Under the Bolsonaro administration, enforcement was weakened. The Lula government (from Jan 2023) has reinvigorated MTE inspections and Lista Suja additions. Government response sub-scores expected to improve in next Walk Free cycle. Structural vulnerability (47) remains high due to Amazon agricultural sector and garment informal economy, but government response trajectory is genuinely improving.
monitoring_signals: MTE inspection statistics and Lista Suja additions by sector; ILO Brazil country programme reports; Amazon deforestation/labour enforcement correlation data; GOJO Brazil facility-level audit data

key_risk_driver: Bill S-211 (Fighting Against Forced Labour and Child Labour in Supply Chains Act) in force since January 2024; government response sub-score for supply chain due diligence improving
context: Bill S-211 requires annual reporting from ~4,000+ Canadian businesses on forced labour and child labour risks in supply chains. As the first mandatory supply chain reporting legislation in Canada, it directly improves the Walk Free sub-score for 'government and business stop sourcing goods produced by forced labour.' Low structural vulnerability (11). Tier 1 status well-established.
monitoring_signals: S-211 annual report submission rates and quality; CBSA anti-trafficking enforcement statistics; any proposed strengthening of S-211 to include mandatory due diligence (not just reporting)

overall_risk_trajectory: Tier 3 – No improvement anticipated; XUAR-specific risk intensifying
key_risk_driver: State-sponsored Xinjiang labour transfer programs ongoing; Tier 3 status with zero institutional improvement mechanism; UFLPA enforcement escalating
context: China's Tier 3 status reflects documented state-sponsored forced labour programs in Xinjiang (XUAR), absence of ILO Convention 29 ratification, and no independent monitoring access. Walk Free government response score (40) is at a structural low with no credible improvement pathway. US CBP UFLPA enforcement is expanding — Entity List additions continuing in 2024-25. UN OHCHR August 2022 assessment found XUAR practices may constitute crimes against humanity. For TTI Dongguan and other CHN facilities: even outside XUAR, the broader institutional environment (no independent labour unions, restricted civil society) creates structural vulnerability elevation.
monitoring_signals: UFLPA Entity List additions affecting Chinese manufacturers; CBP withhold/release orders; UN OHCHR and Special Rapporteur follow-up assessments on XUAR; any Chinese ILO Convention 29 ratification signals

overall_risk_trajectory: Tier 3 – Stable; no imminent tier change

context: Vietnam's Tier 3 classification reflects persistent broker fee systems creating debt bondage for workers migrating to South Korea, Japan, Taiwan; documented forced labour in fishing vessels and export garment/electronics manufacturing. overall_risk_trajectory: Tier 2 – Watchlist risk if conflict continues to divert government capacity
key_risk_driver: Gaza conflict (Oct 2023 ongoing) creating acute vulnerability pressure: Walk Free 'effects of conflict' and 'disenfranchised groups' sub-scores worsening materially; government response capacity partially diverted to wartime operations
context: Israel's vulnerability score (35) was assessed pre-October 2023 conflict. The ongoing conflict is materially worsening sub-scores: internal displacement, agricultural and construction labor market disruption (Palestinian worker access suspended), and increased dependence on migrant workers from Southeast Asia under conditions of reduced oversight. Government response capacity is being absorbed by conflict operations. Perrigo Israel (Yeruham) sits in the Negev region somewhat distant from conflict zones but within the same institutional context. Confidence is Low given genuine uncertainty about conflict duration and resolution.
monitoring_signals: Conflict duration and ceasefire trajectory; ILO Israel assessments; migrant worker permit data (construction sector); Walk Free 2027 Israel vulnerability sub-score for 'effects of conflict' and 'disenfranchised groups'

overall_risk_trajectory: Tier 2 – Stable; USMCA RRM increasing scrutiny pressure

context: Mexico's vulnerability score (58) is the highest in the dataset. Active cartel involvement in forced labour recruitment (particularly in Jalisco, Tamaulipas, and Chihuahua manufacturing corridors — relevant to Torreon/Toluca facilities) documented by ILAB. USMCA Chapter 23 Rapid Response Mechanism has flagged labour rights concerns at Mexican manufacturing plants. Sheinbaum government maintaining social investment but rule of law indicators (criminal justice sub-scores) under continued organised crime pressure.
monitoring_signals: USMCA Rapid Response Mechanism petitions filed against Mexican facilities; STPS labour inspection data in manufacturing zones; cartel activity reports in Coahuila and State of Mexico regions

overall_risk_trajectory: Tier 2 – Stable; agricultural caporalato system remains a concern but contained
key_risk_driver: EU membership and CSDDD transposition provide institutional floor; agricultural caporalato (gang-master) system in Campania/Apulia a persistent concern but not sufficient to affect cluster

overall_risk_trajectory: Tier 2 – Stable; significant efforts noted but enforcement gap persists
key_risk_driver: High structural vulnerability (56) driven by bonded labour in brick kilns, stone quarries, and agricultural sectors; Tier 2 status reflects significant efforts with wide enforcement gap
context: India's vulnerability score (56) is the second highest in the dataset after Mexico. The Walk Free framework captures high inequality, large disenfranchised populations (Dalits, tribal communities, internal migrants), and governance challenges in enforcement. BLSA (Bonded Labour System Abolition Act) enforcement remains inconsistent across states. ZURU India facilities are software/technology sites (Leave blank Software) which have lower direct modern slavery exposure within the facility itself, but the country score applies to the operational context and supply chain. No material change in either direction expected within 12 months.
monitoring_signals: National Human Rights Commission (NHRC) bonded labour reports; NCLP enforcement statistics; Walk Free India vulnerability sub-score for bonded labour and governance; ZURU India supplier due diligence programme
